title: בּױגעזאנג | Baugesang (Building Song): a German adaptation of the piyyuṭ Adir Hu attested by Paul Christian Kirchner in his Jüdisches Ceremoniel (Nuremberg, 1717)
author: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (transcription & naqdanut)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also translates and authors his own original works.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Isaac Gantwerk Mayer (translation)
coauthors.bio: From a family of musicians, Isaac Gantwerk Mayer believes that creative art is one of the most powerful ways to get in touch with the divine. He composes music and poetry in Hebrew and English. (He also authors his own original works and transcribes Hebrew and Aramaic text, adding niqqud and t'amim as needed.) Isaac runs a Jewish music transcription service, which will transcribe and set any Jewish music in any language, recorded or written. Contact his service on Facebook or via his music blog.

coauthors.name: Paul Christian Kirchner (translation)
coauthors.bio: Paul Christian Kirchner (born Gumpel ben Mordechai; ca. 1675-?), born in Krotszyn, Poland was an educated Jewish convert to Christianity. From a rabbinic family, he claimed to have been a rabbi for about eight years before his conversion. He was baptized in November 1713 in the Lutheran church in Bischleben in the presence of eleven godparents from the upper classes of Dutchy of Gotha and the city of Erfurt, indicating the prestigiousness of the event. Kircher then moved to Jena where he matriculated at the Lutheran university. He had hoped to earn a living as lecturer in Hebrew and was given a letter of recommendation for the University of Jena but he was unable to gain a permanent foothold in academia. The search for employment took him across Europe, from Königsberg to Basel. The work took a critical stance on Judaism but which was later republished by Sebastian Jugendres with more neutral text. It is generally assumed, although not proven, that he returned to Judaism. At least fifteen editions of his Jüdisches Ceremoniel were printed between 1716 and 1737. (From the article by Maria Diemling in the Journal of Early Modern Christianity 2025; 12(1), pp. 77-78.)

coauthors.name: Unknown
coauthors.bio: Sometimes the best we can do in attributing a historical work is to indicate the period and place it was written, the first prayer book it may have been printed in, or the archival collection in which the manuscript was found. We invite the public to help to attribute all works to their original composers. If you know something not mentioned in the commentary offered, please leave a comment or contact us.

excerpt: This is a variation of the "Baugesang," a variation of the piyyut Adir Hu sung in German translation. Better known in form found in the 1769 Selig Haggadah, this is a variation published in 1717 by Paul Christian Kirchner.
content:
Born into Judaism, Paul Christian Kirchner was baptized a Christian in 1713. In 1717, he first published the work Jüdisches Ceremoniel (or “Jewish Ceremony”), for which he is most well known — a firsthand recollection (and, to be honest, criticism) of Jewish practices throughout the year and life cycle. While the work is written by an apostate for a Christian audience, and thus it is certainly not an unbiased source — and it had been almost a decade since he left the community so some of his memories are a bit muddled — it is still a fascinating work if only for its depiction of Jewish practices otherwise forgotten. In this portion of Kirchner’s description of the Seder night, he translates three songs — Adir Hu, Eḥad Mi Yodea, and Ḥad Gadya — still sung in Jewish communities the world over to this day! This is Adir Hu in a German adaptation known as the “Baugesang,” more well known in its standardized form found in the 1769 Selig Haggadah.

Note: For clarity, we have replaced the use of the Solidus (a/k/a “forward slash”) as found in the Fraktur script of the printed work, with its equivalent — the comma.

Source translation (German) Translation (Modern German) Translation (English) Abridged Source (Hebrew)
Die Ceremonien, welche gebraucht werden bey den zwey Oster-Nächten mit grosser Andacht gebetten und vollbracht , und lauten also auß ihrem Kabala:
Die Zeremonien, welche gebraucht werden bei den zwei Osternächten mit großer Andacht gebeten und vollbracht, und lauten also aus ihrem Kabbala:
The ceremonies required during the two paschal[1] Literally: “Easter.” Uncommon in modern German usage, at the time both Passover and Easter were referred to as such. This is still the case in many languages — see Greek Πάσχα των Ιουδαίων, French Pâque juive, Finnish juutalainen pääsiäinen, and Spanish pascua de los judíos. Note, though, that in all three of those languages, the word for Easter derives from the Hebrew פֶּסַח via Aramaic, which is not the case in German — or in English.  nights, which are solemnly prayed and performed, are as follows from their Kabbalah:
Allmächtiger Gott,
nun bau dein Tempel Schyro
also in Schier und alsobald
in unseren Tagen Schyro
ja Schiro.
Allmächtiger Gott,
nun bau’ dein Tempel schiere,
also in schier und also bald,
in unseren Tagen schiere,
ja schiere!
Almighty God,
now build Your Temple soon,
both soon and also quick,
in our days, soon,
yeah, soon!
אַדִּיר הוּא
יִבְנֶה בֵּיתוֹ בְּקָרוֹב
בִּמְהֵרָה, בִּמְהֵרָה,
בְּיָמֵינוּ בְּקָרוֹב
(אֵל בְּנֵה בֵּיתְךָ) בְּקָרוֹב׃
Barmhaffter GOtt,
grosser GOtt,
demühtiger GOtt,
hoher GOtt,
feiner GOtt,
Süsser und demühtiger GOtt,
Jüdischer GOtt,
nun bau dein Tempel Schyro
also Schier und alsobald,
und baue dein Tempel Schiro.
Barmherziger Gott,
großer Gott,
demütiger Gott,
hoher Gott,
feiner Gott,
süßer und demütiger Gott,
jüdischer Gott,
nun bau’ dein Tempel schiere
also schier und also bald,
und bau’ dein Tempel schiere!
Merciful God,
great God,
humble God,
high God,
nice God,
sweet and humble God,
Jewish God,
now build Your Temple soon,
both soon and also quick,
and build Your Temple soon!
בָּחוּר הוּא
גָּדוֹל הוּא
דָּגוּל הוּא
הָדוּר הוּא
וָתִיק הוּא
זַכַּאי הוּא חָסִיד הוּא…
יָחִיד הוּא
יִבְנֶה בֵּיתוֹ בְּקָרוֹב
בִּמְהֵרָה, בִּמְהֵרָה,
אֵל בְּנֵה בֵּיתְךָ בְּקָרוֹב׃
Kräfftiger lebendiger mächtiger
Namhaffter ewiger forchtsammer
reicher Königlicher waherer
schöner GOtt,
nun bau dein Tempel Schyro also schier
und alsobald
in unseren Tägen[2] Probably a typo for Tagen  Schiro.
Nun baue dein Tempel Schiro.
Kräftiger lebendiger mächtiger
namhafter ewiger furchtsamer
reicher königlicher wahrer
schöner Gott,
nun bau’ dein Tempel schiere
also und also bald,
in unseren Tagen schiere,
nun bau’ dein Tempel schiere!
Strong, living, mighty,
renowned, eternal, awesome,
sovereign, royal, true,
beautiful God,
now build Your Temple soon,
both soon and also quick,
in our days, soon,
and build Your Temple soon!
כַּבִּיר הוּא לָמוּד הוּא מֶלֶךְ הוּא
נָאוֹר הוּא…עִזּוּז הוּא פּוֹדֶה הוּא…
קָדוֹשׁ הוּא רַחוּם הוּא צַדִּיק הוּא[3] The divine attribute/epithet corresponding to “wahrer” (true) in the original Hebrew is unclear and my associating it with “tsaddiq” is speculative given the deviation from the original alphabetic acrostic. As “tahor” (pure) was skipped earlier in the acrostic, perhaps that is the correct corresponding attribute. –Ahaon Varady 
שַׁדַּי הוּא…
יִבְנֶה בֵּיתוֹ בְּקָרוֹב
בִּמְהֵרָה, בִּמְהֵרָה,
בְּיָמֵינוּ בְּקָרוֹב.
אֵל בְּנֵה בֵּיתְךָ בְּקָרוֹב׃

This is the early piyyut “Adir Hu” in a German variant of its Western Yiddish adaptation “Baugensang” as attested by Paul Christian Kirchner in his Jüdisches Ceremoniel (1717), p. 127. For comparison, I have added the text of Adir Hu as it would correspond to this variant of the Baugesang. The alphabetic acrostic of the original piyyut is abridged in this translation. As the acrostic is broken in several places, it is unclear whether this variant itself is abbreviated or whether the translation is abridged. All elisions in the Baugesang are indicated with elipses in the Hebrew text of Adir Hu. –Aharon Varady

Source(s)

Jüdisches Ceremoniel (Paul Christian Kirchner, 1717), p. 127

 

Notes

Notes
1 Literally: “Easter.” Uncommon in modern German usage, at the time both Passover and Easter were referred to as such. This is still the case in many languages — see Greek Πάσχα των Ιουδαίων, French Pâque juive, Finnish juutalainen pääsiäinen, and Spanish pascua de los judíos. Note, though, that in all three of those languages, the word for Easter derives from the Hebrew פֶּסַח via Aramaic, which is not the case in German — or in English.
2 Probably a typo for Tagen
3 The divine attribute/epithet corresponding to “wahrer” (true) in the original Hebrew is unclear and my associating it with “tsaddiq” is speculative given the deviation from the original alphabetic acrostic. As “tahor” (pure) was skipped earlier in the acrostic, perhaps that is the correct corresponding attribute. –Ahaon Varady
